representations.raw:
Section 4.2 — Professional Development Reimbursement.

Full-time employees may request reimbursement for external courses, certifications, books, and conference registrations that are directly related to their current role or to a documented career-development path approved by their manager. The maximum reimbursement per calendar year is $2,000 per employee. Reimbursement requests must be submitted through the Workday portal within 60 days of the original purchase, accompanied by a paid receipt and a one-paragraph statement of relevance written by the employee.

Section 4.3 — Language Training.

Language courses, language certification exams, and immersion programs are reimbursable under Section 4.2 only when the employee's manager and the People Operations team have jointly pre-approved the course in writing before enrollment. This pre-approval requirement applies to all language-related expenses regardless of amount, regardless of role, and regardless of whether the language is used in the employee's current work. Pre-approval is granted via the Language Learning Request form, which must include a business-justification statement and confirmation from the employee's manager.

gold_answer.full: No, not through the standard Workday submission alone. Section 4.3 applies regardless of amount, role, and language-to-role relevance, so the standard Section 4.2 path is not sufficient. You should submit the Language Learning Request form before enrolling, obtain both written approvals, and then proceed with enrollment and post-course Workday submission.

representations.raw:
Standard Ticket Handling Procedure.

All inbound support requests, bug reports, and customer-impact issues should be filed in the Engineering Jira project under the appropriate component. Tickets enter the queue at priority Triage and are reviewed by the Level-1 support engineer on shift within four business hours. Level-1 confirms reproducibility, gathers customer context, and either resolves the issue or escalates to Level-2 with a written summary. Level-2 engineers handle backend and integration issues and may escalate to Level-3 (component owners) for changes requiring code modifications. Standard turnaround for full resolution under this flow is two to five business days depending on complexity.

Major Incident Procedure.

When an event meets the criteria for a Severity-1 major incident — defined as full or partial outage of a customer-facing production service, data-integrity issue affecting more than one customer, or any security breach — the standard ticket flow is suspended and the Major Incident Procedure takes effect. The on-call incident commander must be paged immediately through PagerDuty using the incident-commander escalation policy. The incident commander then assembles the response team, declares the incident in the #incident-active Slack channel, and assumes coordination authority. Filing a Jira ticket is still required for post-incident review purposes, but it must not delay paging. Any time spent in the standard triage queue during a Severity-1 event is treated as a process failure in post-mortem.

representations.raw:
Childcare Subsidy Program.

The company offers a monthly childcare subsidy of $400 per eligible child to help employees offset the cost of daycare, preschool, and after-school care. The subsidy is paid directly into payroll on the 15th of each month and does not require receipts. Employees may claim the subsidy for up to two children simultaneously.

Eligibility is restricted to permanent full-time employees on the standard employment contract who have completed their initial probationary period of ninety days. Independent contractors, fractional consultants, interns, and employees on fixed-term project contracts are not eligible for this benefit, regardless of tenure or hours worked. The subsidy applies only to children between the ages of zero and five years inclusive at the time of the subsidy month; in the month a child turns six, eligibility for that child ends. Enrollment is handled through the People Operations portal and requires upload of the child's birth certificate.

<scope id="S1" bounds="R1" dimension="employment_type">Eligibility is restricted to permanent full-time employees on the standard employment contract who have completed their initial probationary period of ninety days. Independent contractors, fractional consultants, interns, and employees on fixed-term project contracts are not eligible for this benefit, regardless of tenure or hours worked.</scope>

<scope id="S2" bounds="R1" dimension="child_age">The subsidy applies only to children between the ages of zero and five years inclusive at the time of the subsidy month; in the month a child turns six, eligibility for that child ends.</scope>

<rule id="R2">Enrollment is handled through the People Operations portal and requires upload of the child's birth certificate.</rule>

representations.raw:
Accounts Payable — Vendor Invoice Processing.

Accounts Payable processes vendor invoices through the standard Net-30 payment cycle. Approved invoices submitted by 5:00 PM on a business day are batched for payment that evening and reach the vendor's account within thirty calendar days of the invoice date. Invoice approval is performed by the project owner on record in NetSuite.

For first-time payments to any new vendor, two onboarding steps must be completed before the first invoice may be released for payment. First, the vendor must pass the third-party due-diligence review conducted by the Compliance team, which includes sanctions screening, beneficial-ownership disclosure, and tax-form validation. Compliance issues a formal Due Diligence Approval memo when the review is complete. Second, a signed Master Services Agreement must be in place between the company and the vendor, executed by the vendor's authorized signatory and by the company's Procurement Director. Both onboarding steps must be complete before the first payment is released. Subsequent invoices from the same vendor follow the standard Net-30 cycle without these onboarding checks, unless the vendor relationship has been dormant for more than eighteen months, in which case both steps must be repeated.

Invoice approval is performed by the project owner on record in NetSuite.</rule>

<precondition id="P1" required_for="R1 when vendor_status=first_payment" condition="vendor has passed third-party due-diligence review">For first-time payments to any new vendor, the vendor must pass the third-party due-diligence review conducted by the Compliance team, which includes sanctions screening, beneficial-ownership disclosure, and tax-form validation. Compliance issues a formal Due Diligence Approval memo when the review is complete.</precondition>

<precondition id="P2" required_for="R1 when vendor_status=first_payment" condition="signed Master Services Agreement is in place">A signed Master Services Agreement must be in place between the company and the vendor, executed by the vendor's authorized signatory and by the company's Procurement Director.</precondition>

<rule id="R2" relates_to="P1, P2">Both onboarding steps must be complete before the first payment is released. Subsequent invoices from the same vendor follow the standard Net-30 cycle without these onboarding checks, unless the vendor relationship has been dormant for more than eighteen months, in which case both steps must be repeated.</rule>

query: We are about to release the first invoice payment to Helix Analytics, a new vendor we engaged for a quarterly data audit. The invoice is for $42,000 and the project owner has approved it in NetSuite. Compliance issued the Due Diligence Approval memo for Helix two weeks ago. The Master Services Agreement has been negotiated and the vendor's signatory countersigned it last Thursday, but our Procurement Director has been on leave and has not yet signed; her countersignature is expected next Tuesday. Can Accounts Payable release the payment in tonight's batch to keep us within Net-30, given that we will have the Procurement Director's signature within five business days?

gold_answer.full: No — Accounts Payable should not release the payment in tonight's batch. This is a first-time payment to Helix Analytics, which gates release on two preconditions that must both be complete. Precondition P1 (Due Diligence Approval) is satisfied — the Compliance memo was issued two weeks ago. Precondition P2 (signed Master Services Agreement) is not yet satisfied because the company's Procurement Director has not countersigned; a vendor-only signature does not constitute an executed agreement under the policy, which requires execution by both the vendor's authorized signatory and the company's Procurement Director. Net-30 timing pressure does not relieve the precondition. The correct action is to hold the payment until the Procurement Director countersigns next Tuesday, and release in the batch on or after that date.
gold_answer.minimal: No — first-payment release requires BOTH preconditions; the MSA is not fully executed because the company's Procurement Director has not countersigned. Net-30 pressure does not waive this.
required_relations: P1 AND P2 are both required for first payment
partial satisfaction of P2 (one-sided signature) does not satisfy P2

representations.raw:
Production Deployment Runbook — Schema-Changing Releases.

This runbook applies to any release that includes a database migration affecting the public schema of the primary application database. A schema-changing release consists of three coordinated steps: applying the migration to the primary database via the Liquibase changelog, deploying the new application code to the production fleet via Spinnaker, and updating the feature-flag configuration in LaunchDarkly. The release engineer on shift owns end-to-end coordination.

The migration must be applied before the code is deployed. Application code that depends on new columns or new tables will throw schema errors against the old database state, causing 500-level responses to customers until the migration completes. For this reason, the schema migration is always run first; only after Liquibase reports a successful migration and the post-migration health check passes does the release engineer trigger the Spinnaker pipeline for the application deploy. Feature-flag updates are the final step and are typically gradual rollouts starting at five percent of traffic.

If a rollback becomes necessary, the order is reversed. The application code must be rolled back to the previous version first, restoring compatibility with the old schema; only then is the migration rolled back. Rolling back the migration while the new code is still running on the fleet will produce the same schema-error symptom in the opposite direction. Feature flags should be disabled at the first sign of trouble, but flag changes alone are not a substitute for code or schema rollback when the issue is at the data layer.

query: We are deploying release 4.18 tonight at 22:00. The release includes a Liquibase migration that adds two columns to the orders table and the application code that reads those columns. The deploy window is tight and the on-call team is small. gold_answer.full: No — this plan is not safe and contradicts the forward deployment sequence in the runbook. The migration must be applied first, and only after Liquibase reports success and the post-migration health check passes does the code deploy begin. Running them in parallel exposes a window during which the new application code is running against the old schema (because the code rollout may complete before, or simultaneously with, the migration), and any traffic hitting code paths that reference the new columns will produce 500-level responses until the migration catches up. The fact that the two tools target different systems is precisely what makes the ordering matter — the code reads the schema, so the schema must be ahead of the code. The correct sequence is: (1) apply migration via Liquibase and wait for success plus health check, (2) trigger Spinnaker for code deploy, (3) update LaunchDarkly feature flag at 5% traffic.

representations.raw:
PostgreSQL Tuning Guidance — Read-Heavy Workloads.

For production PostgreSQL instances serving read-heavy analytical workloads, the parallel-query feature significantly improves performance on large sequential scans, hash joins, and aggregate operations. The default value of max_parallel_workers_per_gather is 2, which is conservative and leaves most modern hardware underutilized. For instances running on dedicated database servers with sufficient resources, raising max_parallel_workers_per_gather to 8 typically delivers a two-to-fourfold reduction in query latency for analytical queries that the planner determines are eligible for parallelization. The setting can be changed at the session level for testing or at the postgresql.conf level for permanent application.

This tuning is not appropriate for all environments. Each parallel worker consumes its own work_mem allocation for hash and sort operations, and on instances with constrained memory the multiplication of work_mem across parallel workers can exhaust available RAM. Specifically, raising max_parallel_workers_per_gather above 4 on instances with less than 16 GB of total system RAM is known to cause out-of-memory failures, query cancellations, and in repeated cases full PostgreSQL crashes under sustained analytical load. The default value of 2, or a maximum of 4 on smaller instances, should be retained until system RAM exceeds 16 GB. Memory-constrained instances should instead focus on query rewriting, index tuning, and connection pooling before considering parallelism increases.

The general tuning recommendation in R1 applies to instances with sufficient resources, but C1 contraindicates raising the value above 4 on instances with less than 16 GB of total system RAM. The composition challenge is twofold: (a) recognizing that a date attached to a case (departure date) determines which regime applies, and (b) recognizing that the new regime is not retroactive. representations.raw:
Employee Records Retention Policy.

Prior to 1 July 2025, the company retained all personnel records — including performance reviews, training history, internal communications, and contract-related documents — for a period of seven years after the employee's departure date. This was the single retention period applicable to all record categories under the prior policy.

Effective 1 July 2025, the Updated Records Retention Policy took effect. For employees whose departure date is on or after 1 July 2025, personal records (performance reviews, training history, internal communications) are retained for three years after departure. Contract-related and tax-related documents (signed employment contract, payroll history, tax filings) continue to be retained for seven years for fiscal-compliance reasons. The updated policy thus distinguishes between two record categories with two different retention periods.

The updated policy applies prospectively only. Employees whose departure date is before 1 July 2025 remain under the prior policy: a single seven-year retention period applies to all record categories, with no category distinction. The People Operations team does not reclassify pre-cutover records retroactively. The cutover date refers to the employee's actual departure date as recorded in Workday, not to the date a record was created or the date a retention question is asked.

representations.raw:
Procurement Policy — Software Acquisition.

Department managers may approve software purchases for their team up to an annual total of $10,000 per vendor, provided the spend is within the team's approved budget for the fiscal year. Standard approval is registered in the Procurement Portal with a brief justification and the vendor invoice. Approval is reflected in NetSuite within one business day.

Software that processes customer-identifiable data — including personally identifiable information, payment data, and authentication credentials — requires independent review and written approval from the Information Security team (the CISO's office), regardless of the purchase amount and regardless of whether the purchase falls within the manager's standard authority. The Security review assesses vendor security posture, data-flow exposure, sub-processor disclosures, and compliance with the company's data-processing requirements. Purchases requiring Security review may take up to fifteen business days for completion.

Software listed in the Pre-Vetted Vendor Catalog does not require Security review on a per-purchase basis, even when the software processes customer-identifiable data. Inclusion in the Pre-Vetted Vendor Catalog requires a one-time Security review by the CISO's office, after which the vendor is added to the catalog and is exempt from per-purchase Security review for a renewable two-year period. The catalog is maintained by Procurement and is accessible to all managers in the Procurement Portal under 'Approved Vendors'. Managers must still register the purchase normally in the Procurement Portal; the catalog exemption removes the Security-review requirement but not the registration requirement. The catalog exemption is invalidated if the vendor's catalog entry has expired or has been revoked by the CISO's office, in which case standard Security review applies for any new purchase.

query: I want to purchase 15 annual licenses of Lumen Analytics for my team for a total of $4,200. Lumen processes customer event data that includes user identifiers and IP addresses (which fall under personally identifiable information). I checked the Procurement Portal under 'Approved Vendors' and Lumen has been in the Pre-Vetted Vendor Catalog since March; the entry is current and shows no revocation flag. The purchase is within my team's FY budget. Can I approve this purchase as the manager via the Procurement Portal, or do I need to send it for Security review first?

gold_answer.full: You can approve this purchase as the manager via the Procurement Portal. No Security review is required. The reasoning runs through all three layers of the policy: (1) The $4,200 amount is within your $10,000 standard authority and within your team's FY budget, satisfying R1. (2) Lumen processes customer-identifiable data (user identifiers, IP addresses constituting PII), which would normally trigger E1 and require CISO Security review regardless of amount. (3) However, Lumen is currently listed in the Pre-Vetted Vendor Catalog with a valid, non-revoked entry — which triggers E1_of_E1 and overrides E1, returning the purchase to the standard R1 path. You still need to register the purchase in the Procurement Portal with a brief justification and the vendor invoice (the catalog exemption removes the Security-review requirement, not the registration requirement). The approval will be reflected in NetSuite within one business day.

metadata.notes: Level-3 chained relations. The case combines four relation types that must all be composed: scope (S1 - employment type), threshold-based precondition (P1 - tenure with a temporal version), amount-based precondition (P2 - extra approver above threshold), and temporal dependency (T1 - interest rate cutover). The primary relation_type label is 'exception' because P2 effectively functions as an exception to the standard approval flow when the amount exceeds the threshold, but the case is intentionally multi-relational. A correct answer requires touching all four. The most pedagogically interesting failure modes are partial composition — getting two or three of the four relations right while missing one.

representations.raw:
Education Loan Program — Internal Policy.

The company operates an internal Education Loan Program to help employees finance approved continuing-education and degree programs. Loans are offered up to a maximum principal of $20,000 with a repayment term of 36 months. Loan disbursement occurs within ten business days of full approval. Repayment is handled through payroll deduction.

Eligibility is restricted to permanent full-time employees on the standard employment contract. Independent contractors, fractional consultants, interns, and employees on fixed-term project contracts are not eligible for the program, regardless of tenure.

Before submitting an application, the employee must have completed a minimum tenure with the company. Until 30 June 2025, the minimum-tenure requirement was twelve months of continuous full-time employment. Effective 1 July 2025, the minimum-tenure requirement was raised to twenty-four months of continuous full-time employment, applied to all new applications submitted on or after that date. Applications submitted before 1 July 2025 are evaluated against the prior twelve-month requirement, even if the approval process extends into the post-cutover period.

For loan amounts of $10,000 or less, the employee's direct manager is the sole required approver. For loan amounts above $10,000, both the direct manager and the Chief Financial Officer must approve in writing; the CFO approval is required regardless of the employee's role, department, or business unit. This dual-approval requirement does not change the maximum principal limit.

Interest rates on Education Loan Program loans were set at 2.0 percent per annum on the disbursed principal until 30 June 2025. Effective 1 July 2025, the rate was raised to 4.0 percent per annum for all applications submitted on or after that date, in line with updated treasury benchmarks. Applications submitted before 1 July 2025 retain the prior 2.0 percent rate for the full life of the loan, including for disbursements that occur after the cutover, provided the application was complete and on file before the cutover.

representations.raw:
Production Change Management Policy.

Production changes must be approved by the Change Advisory Board (CAB) before deployment. CAB meets twice weekly (Tuesday and Thursday afternoons) to review pending change requests, assess risk, and grant or deny approval. Approved changes are scheduled into the next available maintenance window and executed by the relevant team. After execution, a Post-Implementation Review (PIR) is filed within five business days documenting outcome, incidents, and lessons learned.

This policy applies to changes affecting Tier-1 systems (revenue-critical services such as checkout, payment processing, and authentication) and Tier-2 systems (customer-facing services such as the product catalog, customer dashboard, and notification system). Changes affecting Tier-3 systems (internal-only services such as the engineering wiki, the internal analytics dashboard, and developer tooling) do not require CAB review and are governed by team-level processes.

A Change Request must be submitted in ServiceNow with the proposed change, risk assessment, rollback plan, and target maintenance window at least five business days before the intended execution date. Requests submitted with shorter lead time are deferred to the following CAB cycle unless an exception applies.

The normal change flow proceeds in the following sequence: CAB approval is obtained first, then the approved change is scheduled into a maintenance window, then the change is executed by the team during that window, and finally the PIR is filed within five business days of execution. No step in this sequence may be skipped in normal operations.

When a Severity-1 incident is active and a production change is the corrective action required to resolve the incident, the normal change flow is suspended for that specific change. The change may be applied immediately by the on-call team without prior CAB approval and without the five-business-day lead time. This emergency authorization applies regardless of system tier and regardless of normal CAB cadence. The emergency change must be accompanied by a post-hoc CAB review filed within forty-eight hours of execution, which retroactively assesses the change, captures rollback decisions, and feeds into the standard PIR process. Failure to file the post-hoc review within forty-eight hours is treated as a process violation in the incident post-mortem.

query: The checkout service has been failing for thirteen minutes — Severity-1 declared, on-call paged, customer impact ongoing. Our diagnosis points to a misconfigured connection-pool setting that was deployed two days ago. The proposed corrective action is a configuration rollback to the prior known-good value, applied directly to the production fleet. We have not submitted a Change Request in ServiceNow and there is no CAB meeting scheduled for two more days. Two questions: (a) Can the on-call team apply the rollback now, before submitting a Change Request and without CAB approval? (b) Are there any further policy obligations after the rollback is applied?

gold_answer.full: (a) Yes — the on-call team can apply the rollback now, without prior CAB approval and without satisfying the five-business-day lead time. A Severity-1 incident is active with ongoing customer impact, and the proposed change (configuration rollback to the known-good value) is the corrective action for that active incident. This activates E1, which supersedes R1 (CAB-prior-approval), P1 (five-business-day lead time), and SEQ1 (normal flow ordering) for this specific change. The override applies regardless of system tier (checkout is Tier-1 but tier does not block the override) and regardless of the CAB schedule. (b) Yes — applying the change is not the end of the obligation. A post-hoc CAB review must be filed within forty-eight hours of execution, retroactively assessing the change, capturing rollback decisions, and feeding into the standard PIR process. Failure to file the post-hoc review within that forty-eight-hour window is itself treated as a process violation in the incident post-mortem. The PIR (per R1's standard five-business-day window for documenting outcome) still applies on the normal cadence.
gold_answer.minimal: Yes, apply the rollback immediately under the emergency override. Required follow-up: file a post-hoc CAB review within 48 hours of execution; standard PIR still applies.

representations.raw:
Residential Maintenance Addendum - Synthetic Lease Training Example.

Section 8. Repair Completion Window. When a tenant submits a written repair notice for a non-emergency habitability item, the property manager must complete the repair within thirty calendar days after receiving the notice. If the repair is not completed within that thirty-day window, the tenant may request a rent credit or begin the lease-termination review process under the addendum.

Section 9. Access for Repairs. The thirty-day repair window does not run during any period in which the tenant refuses or fails to provide reasonable access for the repair. When access is refused or missed, the repair clock pauses on the scheduled access date and resumes only when the tenant provides a new reasonable access window. This pause applies even if the original repair notice was valid and even if the repair would otherwise be covered by Section 8.

Section 3. Covered Incident Reporting. Covered processors must notify the Data Oversight Authority within seventy-two hours after confirming a reportable personal-data breach. Section 4. Sandbox and Masked Data. Incidents limited entirely to masked sandbox data are not reportable to the Data Oversight Authority under Section 3. The applicable reporting window is determined by the incident confirmation date, not by the date the investigation began.

representations.raw:
Ambulatory Infusion Unit Protocol for Fictional Treatment Aurelin.

Section 1. Standard Start. A patient scheduled for Aurelin infusion may start the infusion when identity has been verified, baseline vital signs have been recorded, and the clinician order is active for the current visit. If these three items are complete, nursing staff may begin the infusion under the standard-start pathway.

Section 2. Rescue Kit Requirement. The standard-start pathway is unavailable unless an Aurelin rescue kit is physically present in the infusion bay before the infusion begins. A kit assigned to another bay or stored in the central pharmacy does not satisfy this requirement. If the kit is absent, staff must delay the infusion until a kit is present in the bay.

Section 4. First-Dose Observation. For a patient's first Aurelin infusion only, staff must observe the patient for thirty minutes after the infusion is completed before discharge. The first-dose observation requirement does not prevent starting the infusion if the start preconditions are satisfied; it controls the discharge sequence after completion.

Section 2. Accommodation Eligibility. Students with an active accommodation letter may take approved timed assessments with the Accessibility Testing Center. The letter must list the assessment type and approved time adjustment. A valid letter allows the student to request testing-center administration instead of standard classroom administration.

After the student's active accommodation letter is on file and the instructor exam packet has been uploaded, the Testing Center schedules the appointment and sends confirmation to the student and instructor. The appointment confirmation is the final scheduling step and must not be sent before both inputs are complete.

representations.raw:
Network Firewall Change Playbook.

Section 1. Release Freeze. During a declared network release freeze, non-emergency firewall rule changes to production edge firewalls are blocked. Requests submitted during a freeze are queued for the first standard change window after the freeze ends and must follow ordinary peer review.

Section 3. Active Exploit Override. A firewall rule change that blocks an active externally confirmed exploit may be implemented during a release freeze if the Security Incident Commander declares the change emergency mitigation in the incident channel. The emergency declaration overrides the release-freeze queueing rule only for the firewall rules needed to block the active exploit.

Section 5. Post-Implementation Review. Any firewall change implemented under the active-exploit override must receive peer review and incident-owner sign-off within twenty-four hours after implementation. The post-implementation review does not need to occur before deployment of the emergency mitigation.

representations.raw:
Synthetic Research Incentive Credit Manual.

Section 1. Qualifying Research Expense Rule. A filer may include research expenses in the Synthetic Research Incentive Credit when the work is documented as technical experimentation for a product that the filer intends to commercialize. Qualifying expenses may include employee labor, prototype materials, and third-party development invoices.

Section 2. Related Offshore Contractor Exclusion. Third-party development invoices are excluded when the contractor is both a related party and performs the invoiced work outside the filing jurisdiction. The exclusion applies even if the work is technical experimentation and even if the filer intends to commercialize the product.

Section 3. Cost-Sharing Safe Harbor. The related-offshore-contractor exclusion does not apply if, before the invoiced work begins, the filer and contractor execute a cost-sharing agreement that assigns commercialization rights for the developed intellectual property to the filer. A cost-sharing agreement signed after the work begins does not activate the safe harbor.

Section 6. Technical Uncertainty Memo Cutover. For claims filed on or after 1 March 2026, any third-party development invoice included in the credit must be supported by a technical-uncertainty memo dated no later than the invoice date. Claims filed before 1 March 2026 are not subject to the memo requirement. The cutover is anchored to the claim filing date, not the date the research work began.

representations.raw:
Fictional Ward Protocol for Medication Virexa.

Section 1. Same-Day Discharge Rule. Patients receiving their first Virexa dose may be discharged the same day if they remain clinically stable for two hours after administration and have no active discharge block documented in the chart.

Section 2. Platelet Block. Virexa should not be administered when the platelet count is below 75,000 per microliter. A platelet count below that level is an active discharge block because the first dose has not been validly administered.

Section 3. Hematology Clearance Exception. The platelet block may be cleared only if a hematology consultant documents same-day clearance for Virexa administration in the chart. The clearance applies only to the platelet block and does not waive any other pre-dose safety check.

Section 6. Renal Check Version Rule. Until 30 April 2026, the renal-function check used before first-dose Virexa administration could be up to forty-eight hours old. Effective 1 May 2026, the renal-function check must be collected within twenty-four hours before first-dose administration. The version is selected by the planned administration date, not by the admission date.

representations.raw:
Emergency Housing Stabilization Grant Manual.

Section 1. Basic Eligibility. Households may receive an Emergency Housing Stabilization Grant of up to $3,000 when their primary residence is in the Blue River flood zone and the household was displaced by the declared flood event. Both homeowners and renters may apply.

Section 2. Occupancy Documentation. Renters ordinarily must provide a signed lease showing that the flooded address was their primary residence on the date of the flood. A renter who cannot produce a signed lease may use the alternate-occupancy path if they provide both a utility bill for the flooded address and a school, employment, or clinic record showing the same address.

Section 3. Inspection Precondition. No grant may be approved until a municipal damage inspection has been completed and marked eligible in the grant portal. A pending inspection, a scheduled inspection, or a private contractor estimate does not satisfy this precondition.

Section 5. Photo Evidence Cutover. Applications submitted before 15 September 2026 do not require applicant-supplied photo evidence. For applications submitted on or after 15 September 2026, the applicant must upload at least two photos of flood damage before approval. The photo requirement is based on the application submission date, not on the flood date or inspection date.

representations.raw:
Kubernetes maintenance note.

Section A. Node draining. A cluster operator may drain a node before maintenance by evicting Pods from that node. Draining is treated as a voluntary disruption for workloads whose Pods are evicted through the eviction API.

Section B. PodDisruptionBudget constraint. When a workload has a PodDisruptionBudget that requires zero voluntary evictions, Kubernetes should not evict one of those Pods for an ordinary drain. If the node contains such an unevictable Pod, the drain may not complete until the budget changes, the workload changes, or another non-voluntary condition removes the Pod.

<contraindication id="C1" blocks="R1" condition="PDB permits zero voluntary evictions">Section B. PodDisruptionBudget constraint. When a workload has a PodDisruptionBudget that requires zero voluntary evictions, Kubernetes should not evict one of those Pods for an ordinary drain. If the node contains such an unevictable Pod, the drain may not complete until the budget changes, the workload changes, or another non-voluntary condition removes the Pod.</contraindication>

representations.raw:
Kubernetes scheduling note.

Section A. Priority scheduling. Pods may have priorities. When a pending Pod cannot be scheduled, the scheduler may try to make room by preempting lower-priority Pods.

Section B. Disruption budgets. A PodDisruptionBudget expresses how many Pods of an application should remain available during disruptions. Operators should use PDBs to protect availability during planned voluntary disruptions.

Section C. Preemption interaction. During scheduler preemption, Kubernetes attempts to choose victims in a way that respects PDBs when possible, but respecting a PDB is not guaranteed. If no feasible set of lower-priority victims satisfies both scheduling and PDB preferences, preemption can still select lower-priority Pods whose eviction violates the PDB.
representations.annotated:
Kubernetes scheduling note.

<rule id="R2">Section B. Disruption budgets. A PodDisruptionBudget expresses how many Pods of an application should remain available during disruptions. Operators should use PDBs to protect availability during planned voluntary disruptions.</rule>

<override id="O1" priority="higher_than_R2_for_scheduler_preemption" applies_to="R1">Section C. Preemption interaction. During scheduler preemption, Kubernetes attempts to choose victims in a way that respects PDBs when possible, but respecting a PDB is not guaranteed. If no feasible set of lower-priority victims satisfies both scheduling and PDB preferences, preemption can still select lower-priority Pods whose eviction violates the PDB.</override>

rules:
  - id: R1
    name: scheduler_preemption
    trigger: high_priority_pod_pending_unschedulable
    action: may_preempt_lower_priority_pods
  - id: R2
    name: pod_disruption_budget
    purpose: preserve_application_availability
    normally_applies_to: planned_voluntary_disruptions
overrides:
  - id: O1
    relation: preemption_attempts_to_respect_pdb_but_not_guaranteed
    affects: [R1, R2]

      no_feasible_victim_set_satisfies_scheduling_and_pdb_preferences: true
    result: lower_priority_pod_may_be_preempted_even_if_pdb_is_violated

query: A high-priority Pod is pending because the cluster has no room. The only lower-priority victim candidate is protected by a PDB that would be violated if that Pod were evicted. The scheduler has no other feasible victim set. Does the PDB absolutely prevent scheduler preemption?

gold_answer.full: No. The PDB should be respected when possible, but in scheduler preemption it is a best-effort preference, not an absolute bar. If no feasible victim set both schedules the high-priority Pod and preserves the PDB, the lower-priority Pod may still be preempted.
gold_answer.minimal: No. In scheduler preemption, Kubernetes tries to respect PDBs but may violate one if no feasible alternative exists.
required_relations: override:preemption relation has priority over absolute PDB interpretation
scope:PDB protection is strongest for voluntary disruptions and not guaranteed for scheduler preemption

local_probes.expected_answer: It expresses desired availability during disruptions.

local_probes.expected_answer: No. Kubernetes attempts to respect PDBs when possible, but it is not guaranteed.

representations.raw:
Kubernetes scheduling policy.

Section A. Priority. A Pod with a higher PriorityClass is more important than Pods with lower priority. Higher priority affects ordering in the scheduling queue.

Section B. Default preemption behavior. A high-priority pending Pod can cause lower-priority Pods to be preempted if that is needed to make the pending Pod schedulable.

Section C. Non-preempting PriorityClass. If a PriorityClass sets preemptionPolicy to Never, Pods using that class still get the higher queue priority, but they do not trigger preemption of other Pods. They wait for resources to become available rather than evicting lower-priority Pods.
representations.annotated:
Kubernetes scheduling policy.

<rule id="R1">Section A. Priority. A Pod with a higher PriorityClass is more important than Pods with lower priority. Higher priority affects ordering in the scheduling queue.</rule>

<rule id="R2">Section B. Default preemption behavior. A high-priority pending Pod can cause lower-priority Pods to be preempted if that is needed to make the pending Pod schedulable.</rule>

<exception id="E1" overrides="R2" condition="PriorityClass.preemptionPolicy == Never">Section C. Non-preempting PriorityClass. If a PriorityClass sets preemptionPolicy to Never, Pods using that class still get the higher queue priority, but they do not trigger preemption of other Pods. They wait for resources to become available rather than evicting lower-priority Pods.</exception>

rules:
  - id: R1
    property: higher_priority
    effects: [queue_order_preference]
  - id: R2
    default_preemption: high_priority_pending_pod_may_preempt_lower_priority_pods
exceptions:
  - id: E1
    overrides: R2
    condition:
      preemptionPolicy: Never
    retained_effect: queue_order_preference
    blocked_effect: triggering_preemption

gold_answer.full: No. The Pod keeps its higher scheduling queue priority, but the non-preempting PriorityClass exception blocks the usual preemption behavior. It should wait for resources rather than evicting lower-priority Pods.
gold_answer.minimal: No. preemptionPolicy: Never preserves queue priority but prevents triggering preemption.

StatefulSet update note.

Section A. RollingUpdate. The default StatefulSet update strategy performs automated rolling updates when the Pod template changes.

Section B. OnDelete. When a StatefulSet uses the OnDelete update strategy, the controller does not automatically update existing Pods after a template change. Users must manually delete Pods for the controller to recreate them from the updated template.

representations.raw:
IAM evaluation note.

Section A. Identity-based allow. An identity-based policy can allow a principal to perform an action on a resource.

Section B. Organizational policy intersection. When an account is governed by organization-level controls, the effective permission must also be allowed by the applicable service control or resource control policies. The result is an intersection rather than a simple union.

Section C. Explicit deny. An explicit deny in an applicable policy takes priority over allows. If an identity-based policy allows an action but an applicable SCP explicitly denies that action, the request is denied.
representations.annotated:
IAM evaluation note.

<rule id="R1">Section A. Identity-based allow. An identity-based policy can allow a principal to perform an action on a resource.</rule>

<scope id="S1" applies_to="R1" condition="account governed by organization-level controls">Section B. Organizational policy intersection. When an account is governed by organization-level controls, the effective permission must also be allowed by the applicable service control or resource control policies. The result is an intersection rather than a simple union.</scope>

<override id="O1" overrides="R1" priority="highest" condition="applicable explicit deny">Section C. Explicit deny. An explicit deny in an applicable policy takes priority over allows. If an identity-based policy allows an action but an applicable SCP explicitly denies that action, the request is denied.</override>
representations.compiled:
rules:
  - id: R1
    policy_type: identity_based
    effect: Allow
    action: may_allow_request
  - id: S1
    policy_type: organization_controls
    relation: effective_permissions_are_intersection
    requires_allowed_by: [identity_policy, applicable_scp_or_rcp]
overrides:
  - id: O1
    priority: highest
    condition: explicit_deny_in_any_applicable_policy
    result: Deny
    overrides: [identity_allow, resource_allow, scp_allow]

query: An IAM role has an identity-based policy that allows s3:DeleteObject. The account is under an SCP that explicitly denies s3:DeleteObject. There is no resource-based policy involved. Is the request allowed?

gold_answer.full: No. The identity-based policy alone would allow the action, but effective permissions under organizational controls require the applicable policies to permit the action, and an explicit deny in the SCP overrides the allow. The request is denied.
gold_answer.minimal: Denied. The SCP explicit deny overrides the identity-based allow.
required_relations: scope:effective permissions are intersected with organization controls
override:explicit deny overrides allow

representations.raw:
S3 lifecycle note.

Section A. Lifecycle actions. A bucket can have multiple lifecycle rules that make an object eligible for actions such as transition to another storage class, expiration, or creation of a delete marker.

Section B. Same-day precedence. If multiple lifecycle rules make the same object eligible for more than one action on the same day, S3 follows precedence rules: permanent deletion takes precedence over transition, and transition takes precedence over creating a delete marker.

If multiple lifecycle rules make the same object eligible for more than one action on the same day, S3 follows precedence rules: permanent deletion takes precedence over transition, and transition takes precedence over creating a delete marker.</override>

      - create_delete_marker

representations.raw:
Metformin renal-function note.

Section A. Baseline renal check. Before initiating metformin, obtain an estimated glomerular filtration rate, or eGFR. During therapy, renal function should be monitored at least annually and more frequently in patients at risk for renal impairment.

Section B. eGFR below 30. Metformin is contraindicated in patients whose eGFR is below 30 mL/min/1.73 m2.

Section C. eGFR 30 to 45. Starting metformin is not recommended when eGFR is between 30 and 45 mL/min/1.73 m2, although this category is distinct from the below-30 contraindication.

Section A. Standard adult dosing. Paxlovid has a standard dosing regimen for patients whose renal function is normal or only mildly impaired.

Section B. Renal impairment assessment. Renal impairment increases nirmatrelvir exposure. Before selecting the regimen, determine the patient's eGFR category.

Section C. Moderate impairment. For eGFR from 30 to less than 60 mL/min, the dose should be reduced rather than using the standard regimen.

Section D. Severe impairment. For eGFR below 30 mL/min, including hemodialysis, a reduced dose and reduced dose frequency are used under the label's severe-impairment regimen.

Clozapine ANC note.

Section A. Baseline ANC rule. Before initiating clozapine, a baseline absolute neutrophil count, or ANC, must be obtained. For the general population, baseline ANC must be at least 1500 per microliter.

Section B. BEN exception. For patients with documented Benign Ethnic Neutropenia, the baseline ANC threshold is lower: at least 1000 per microliter.

Section C. Monitoring obligation. During treatment, regular ANC monitoring remains required. The lower BEN threshold changes the initiation threshold but does not remove monitoring obligations.

representations.raw:
IRS online payment plan note.

Section A. Short-term plan. An individual taxpayer may qualify online for a short-term payment plan if the total balance is less than $100,000 in combined tax, penalties, and interest and the taxpayer can pay in full within 180 days.

Section B. Long-term plan. An individual taxpayer may qualify online for a long-term monthly installment agreement if the total balance is less than $50,000 in combined tax, penalties, and interest and returns are filed.

Section C. Continuing charges. Interest and applicable penalties continue to accrue until the balance is paid in full, even when a payment plan is approved.

Section A. Income limit. The used clean vehicle credit has modified adjusted gross income limits that depend on filing status.

Section B. Year selection. For the income test, the taxpayer may use modified AGI from either the year the vehicle is placed in service or the preceding year, whichever is less. If income is below the threshold in one of those two years, the income limit is satisfied.

Section C. Other eligibility. The vehicle must still satisfy separate requirements, including used-vehicle and sale requirements. Passing the income test alone does not prove the whole credit.

representations.raw:
Student visa absence note.

Section A. Short formal leave. A Student Visa holder may take a formal Leave of Absence of up to sixty days without visa impact.

Section B. Completion condition. The no-impact treatment for a formal Leave of Absence depends on the student still being able to complete the degree within the current period of Student Visa permission.

Section C. Longer or incompatible absence. If the absence is longer than the allowed period or prevents completion within current permission, the student should not treat it as a simple no-impact leave under this note.

representations.raw:
HTTP cache-control note.

Section A. Cacheable response metadata. A response may contain metadata such as freshness information that would otherwise allow a cache to reuse the response for a period of time.

Section B. no-store directive. The no-store directive tells caches not to store the request or response. This instruction applies to both private and shared caches and overrides ordinary storage/reuse reasoning for that message.
representations.annotated:
HTTP cache-control note.

This instruction applies to both private and shared caches and overrides ordinary storage/reuse reasoning for that message.</override>

    condition: Cache-Control_contains_no-store

    effect: do_not_store_request_or_response
    overrides: R1

representations.raw:
OAuth authorization error note.

Section A. Authorization error redirect. During an authorization-code request, when the resource owner denies the request or the request fails for a reason that still leaves the authorization server with enough valid client and redirect information, the server can return an error response by redirecting the user-agent back to the client's redirection endpoint.

Section B. Invalid redirect target. If the request is invalid because the redirect URI is missing, invalid, or mismatched, or because the client identifier is invalid, the authorization server must not automatically redirect the user-agent to the invalid redirection URI. It should instead inform the resource owner of the error.

Section C. Code exchange. An authorization code, if issued, is later exchanged at the token endpoint. That later exchange does not retroactively make an invalid authorization redirect target safe.
representations.annotated:
OAuth authorization error note.

<rule id="R1">Section A. Authorization error redirect. During an authorization-code request, when the resource owner denies the request or the request fails for a reason that still leaves the authorization server with enough valid client and redirect information, the server can return an error response by redirecting the user-agent back to the client's redirection endpoint.</rule>

<exception id="E1" overrides="R1" condition="invalid redirect_uri or invalid client_id">Section B. Invalid redirect target. If the request is invalid because the redirect URI is missing, invalid, or mismatched, or because the client identifier is invalid, the authorization server must not automatically redirect the user-agent to the invalid redirection URI. It should instead inform the resource owner of the error.</exception>

<sequence id="Q1" after="R1 or E1">Section C. Code exchange. An authorization code, if issued, is later exchanged at the token endpoint. That later exchange does not retroactively make an invalid authorization redirect target safe.</sequence>

rules:
  - id: R1
    context: authorization_endpoint_error
    default_error_response: redirect_to_client_redirection_endpoint
    requires: enough_valid_client_and_redirect_information

  - id: E1
    overrides: R1
    condition_any: [redirect_uri_missing_invalid_or_mismatched, client_id_invalid]
    result: do_not_auto_redirect_to_invalid_uri
    alternative: inform_resource_owner
sequence:
  - id: Q1
    later_step: token_endpoint_code_exchange
    does_not_repair: invalid_authorization_redirect_uri

query: An OAuth authorization request has an invalid redirect_uri, but the server can detect the error. Should the authorization server redirect the user-agent back to that supplied URI with an error code because authorization errors are usually returned by redirect?

gold_answer.full: No. The general error-redirect behavior is blocked when the redirect URI is invalid or mismatched. The server should not automatically redirect to the invalid URI; it should inform the resource owner instead. A later token exchange does not repair the invalid redirect target.
gold_answer.minimal: No. Invalid redirect_uri triggers the exception: do not redirect to that URI; inform the resource owner.
